title: Information Flow Loops

content.question: Which system are you running without any feedback mechanism?
sop: List the systems or routines you run regularly: marketing, operations, health, finances.
For each, identify what output you could actually measure, not just feel.
Set a fixed review point (weekly, monthly) to look at that measurement, not an occasional glance.
Adjust one specific input based on what the last review showed you.
Close the loop, write down what changed and why, so the next review has something to compare against.
questions: Which system are you running without any feedback mechanism?
What decision are you still making the same way you made it a year ago, with no new data since?
If your last 10 decisions in one area were reviewed together, would you see a pattern of improvement, or the same mistake repeating?

definitions.def: When a system's output is fed back as input, allowing self-correction or self-amplification.

content.equation_example: Current (a performance score you're tracking) plus Feedback Adjustment (the change you make based on reviewing results, positive or negative) equals Next State. Current 60 plus Feedback Adjustment plus 15 equals Next State 75. A rising Next State means the loop is actually improving the system; a flat or falling one means feedback isn't being used, just collected.
content.closing_statement: A system without feedback just repeats its mistakes.

caption:
Principle Nº 191: Information Flow Loops

Effective systems rely on feedback.

Ask yourself: Which system are you running without any feedback mechanism?
